perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by, and construed in accordance with, the laws of the State of ____________________ without giving effect to any conflicts or choice of laws provisions that would cause the application of the domestic substantive laws of any other jurisdiction.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by, and construed in accordance with, the laws of the State of ____________________ without giving effect to any conflicts or choice of laws provisions that would cause the application of the domestic substantive laws of any other jurisdiction. Recipient's obligations under this Agreement with respect to disclosures of Confidential Information made hereunder shall expire five (5) years from the date this Agreement is executed by the Parties.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the 'Term' clause (originally clause 7) into the 'Miscellaneous' section obscures its importance and makes it less likely to be noticed. This affects the enforceability of the confidentiality obligations.
perturbation.law_explanation: UTSA requires reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy. Burying the term limitation within a miscellaneous section undermines the 'reasonable efforts' standard. While a five-year term isn't inherently unreasonable, its placement could be interpreted as a lack of emphasis on maintaining secrecy, potentially impacting enforceability under UTSA. It may also violate UTSA if the term is unreasonably short, given the nature of the confidential information.

perturbation.justification: The perturbation introduces a structural flaw by moving the term clause into the 'Miscellaneous' section. According to the Perturbation Explanation, this obscures its importance and affects the enforceability of confidentiality obligations. The Law Explanation states that this undermines the 'reasonable efforts' standard required by UTSA to maintain secrecy. Although the scraped snippets do not directly address UTSA or the 'reasonable efforts' standard, the Perturbation Type definition explicitly states that a structural flaw creates a legal contradiction when a required legal disclosure is placed in an irrelevant or misleading section. Therefore, by moving the term clause, the modified text creates a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Term. Recipient's obligations under this Agreement with respect to disclosures of Confidential Information made hereunder shall expire five (5) years from the date this Agreement is executed by the Parties.
perturbation.changed_text: Term. Recipient's obligations under this Agreement with respect to disclosures of Confidential Information made hereunder shall expire five (5) years from the date this Agreement is executed by the Parties. Each person who signs this Agreement represents that he/she has the authority and is empowered to sign and deliver this Agreement on behalf of the Recipient that he/she represents.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the 'Authority to Sign' statement (originally located at the end of 'Miscellaneous' section, Section 9) into the 'Term' section buries a crucial aspect of contract validity within a clause that addresses temporal limitations. The authority of the signatory is paramount to the legal standing of the contract, and it should be in a place where it is easily visible.
perturbation.law_explanation: General contract law and agency principles, as outlined in Restatement (Third) of Agency § 2.01, require a valid agreement based on the apparent authority of those signing on behalf of an entity. Relocating this to the “Term” section obscures the core concept of validity. While the clause still exists, its position diminishes its prominence, and hence enforceability, since the term of the contract becomes the focal point rather than the validity of the signatories, potentially leading to challenges regarding whether the signing party had the authority at the time of the signing of the agreement.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 3-403. UNAUTHORIZED SIGNATURE. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 3-403. UNAUTHORIZED SIGNATURE. (a) Unless otherwise provided in this Article or Article 4, an unauthorized signature is ineffective except as the signature of the unauthorized signer in favor of a person who in good faith pays the instrument or takes it for value. An unauthorized signature may be ratified for all purposes of this Article. (b) If the signature of more than one person is required to constitute the authorized signature of an organization, the signature of the organization is unauthorized if one of the required signatures is lacking. (c) The civil or criminal liability of a person who makes an unauthorized signature is not affected by any provision of this Article which makes the unauthorized signature effective for the purposes of this Article. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes

perturbation.justification: The modification introduces a structural flaw by relocating the 'Authority to Sign' clause, which is crucial for contract validity, into the 'Term' section. This placement obscures its importance, potentially leading to enforceability challenges. While UCC § 3-403 addresses unauthorized signatures, the core issue isn't the signature itself being unauthorized, but rather the potential for dispute arising from the obscured placement of the clause affirming signatory authority. The ULC snippet highlights the organization's role in providing clarity to state statutory law, and burying the signature of authority contradicts this principle by obfuscating a fundamental element of contract validity.
